title: Monoamine

infobox.value: are [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") and [neuromodulators](/wiki/Neuromodulator "Neuromodulator") that contain one [amino](/wiki/Amino "Amino") group connected to an [aromatic ring](/wiki/Aromaticity "Aromaticity") by a two-carbon chain (such as -CH2-CH2-). Examples are [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine") and [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Monoamine](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine&redirect=no "Monoamine"))

Monoamine that acts as a neurotransmitter or neuromodulator

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Dopamine.svg/250px-Dopamine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Dopamine.svg)

[Dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Norepinephrine.svg/250px-Norepinephrine.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Norepinephrine.svg)

[Norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Serotonin.svg/250px-Serotonin.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Serotonin.svg)

[Serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin")

**Monoamine neurotransmitters** are [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") and [neuromodulators](/wiki/Neuromodulator "Neuromodulator") that contain one [amino](/wiki/Amino "Amino") group connected to an [aromatic ring](/wiki/Aromaticity "Aromaticity") by a two-carbon chain (such as -CH2-CH2-). Examples are [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine"), [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine") and [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin").

All monoamines are derived from aromatic [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") like [phenylalanine](/wiki/Phenylalanine "Phenylalanine"), [tyrosine](/wiki/Tyrosine "Tyrosine"), and [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan") by the action of [aromatic amino acid decarboxylase](/wiki/Aromatic_amino_acid_decarboxylase "Aromatic amino acid decarboxylase") [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme"). They are deactivated in the body by the enzymes known as [monoamine oxidases](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") which clip off the amine group.

Monoaminergic systems, i.e., the networks of neurons that use monoamine neurotransmitters, are involved in the regulation of processes such as emotion, arousal, and certain types of memory. It has also been found that monoamine neurotransmitters play an important role in the secretion and production of [neurotrophin-3](/wiki/Neurotrophin-3 "Neurotrophin-3") by astrocytes, a chemical which maintains neuron integrity and provides neurons with trophic support.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Drugs used to increase or reduce the effect of monoamine neurotransmitters are used to treat patients with psychiatric and neurological disorders, including [depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder"), [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety_disorder "Anxiety disorder"), [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia") and [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[2]](#cite_note-pubmed21777827-2)

Part of this section is [transcluded](/wiki/Help:Transclusion "Help:Transclusion") from [Trace amine](/wiki/Trace_amine "Trace amine"). ([edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Trace_amine&action=edit) | [history](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Trace_amine&action=history))

Classical monoamines

* [Imidazoleamines](/wiki/Imidazole "Imidazole"):
  + [Histamine](/wiki/Histamine "Histamine")[[6]](#cite_note-pmid18989452-6)
* [Catecholamines](/wiki/Catecholamine "Catecholamine"):
  + [Adrenaline](/wiki/Adrenaline "Adrenaline") (Ad; Epinephrine, Epi)
  + [Dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") (DA)
  + [Noradrenaline](/wiki/Noradrenaline "Noradrenaline") (NAd; Norepinephrine, NE)
* [Indolamines](/wiki/Indolamines "Indolamines"):
  + [Serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") (5-HT)
  + [Melatonin](/wiki/Melatonin "Melatonin") (MT)

Trace amines

* [Phenethylamines](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine") (related to [catecholamines](/wiki/Catecholamine "Catecholamine")):
  + [Phenethylamine](/wiki/Phenethylamine "Phenethylamine")[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Miller-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9) (PEA)
  + [*N*-Methylphenethylamine](/wiki/N-Methylphenethylamine "N-Methylphenethylamine")[[10]](#cite_note-Renaissance_GPCR-10)[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9) (endogenous [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine") isomer)
  + [Phenylethanolamine](/wiki/Phenylethanolamine "Phenylethanolamine")[[11]](#cite_note-TAAR1_ligands-11)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [*m*-Tyramine](/wiki/Meta-Tyramine "Meta-Tyramine")[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [*p*-Tyramine](/wiki/Para-Tyramine "Para-Tyramine")[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [3-Methoxytyramine](/wiki/3-Methoxytyramine "3-Methoxytyramine")[[10]](#cite_note-Renaissance_GPCR-10)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [*N*-Methyltyramine](/wiki/N-Methyltyramine "N-Methyltyramine")[[10]](#cite_note-Renaissance_GPCR-10)[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [*m*-Octopamine](/wiki/Meta-Octopamine "Meta-Octopamine")[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [*p*-Octopamine](/wiki/Para-Octopamine "Para-Octopamine")[[7]](#cite_note-Vascular-7)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
  + [Synephrine](/wiki/Synephrine "Synephrine")[[10]](#cite_note-Renaissance_GPCR-10)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)
* [Tryptamine](/wiki/Tryptamine "Tryptamine")[[10]](#cite_note-Renaissance_GPCR-10)[[8]](#cite_note-Miller-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review-9)

Specific transporter [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") called [monoamine transporters](/wiki/Monoamine_transporter "Monoamine transporter") that transport monoamines in or out of a cell exist. These are the [dopamine transporter](/wiki/Dopamine_transporter "Dopamine transporter") (DAT), [serotonin transporter](/wiki/Serotonin_transporter "Serotonin transporter") (SERT), and the [norepinephrine transporter](/wiki/Norepinephrine_transporter "Norepinephrine transporter") (NET) in the outer [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") and the [vesicular monoamine transporter](/wiki/Vesicular_monoamine_transporter "Vesicular monoamine transporter") ([VMAT1](/wiki/VMAT1 "VMAT1") and [VMAT2](/wiki/VMAT2 "VMAT2")) in the membrane of intracellular [vesicles](/wiki/Vesicle_(biology) "Vesicle (biology)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After release into the [synaptic cleft](/wiki/Synaptic_cleft "Synaptic cleft"), monoamine neurotransmitter action is ended by reuptake into the presynaptic terminal. There, they can be repackaged into synaptic vesicles or degraded by the [enzyme](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") [monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase") (MAO), which is a target of [monoamine oxidase inhibitors](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase_inhibitors "Monoamine oxidase inhibitors"), a class of [antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressants "Antidepressants").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Monoamine_neurotransmitter&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Evolution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Monoamine_receptor_tree.svg/500px-Monoamine_receptor_tree.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Monoamine_receptor_tree.svg)

A phylogenetic tree showing how a number of monoamine receptors are related to each other.

Monoamine neurotransmitter systems occur in virtually all vertebrates, where the evolvability of these systems has served to promote the adaptability of vertebrate species to different environments.[[12]](#cite_note-pmid14597267-12)[[13]](#cite_note-pmid9916344-13)

A recent computational investigation of genetic origins shows that the earliest development of monoamines occurred 650 million years ago and that the appearance of these chemicals, necessary for active or participatory awareness and engagement with the environment, coincides with the emergence of bilaterian or "mirror" body in the midst of (or perhaps in some sense catalytic of?) the Cambrian Explosion.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

6. **[^](#cite_ref-pmid18989452_6-0)** Romero-Calderón R, Uhlenbrock G, Borycz J, Simon AF, Grygoruk A, Yee SK, Shyer A, Ackerson LC, Maidment NT, Meinertzhagen IA, Hovemann BT, Krantz DE (November 2008). ["A glial variant of the vesicular monoamine transporter is required to store histamine in the Drosophila visual system"](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2570955). *PLOS Genet*. **4** (11) e1000245. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1371/journal.pgen.1000245](https://doi.org/10.1371%2Fjournal.pgen.1000245). [PMC](/wiki/PMC_(identifier) "PMC (identifier)") [2570955](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2570955). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [18989452](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18989452). "Unlike other monoamine neurotransmitters, the mechanism by which the brain's histamine content is regulated remains unclear. In mammals, vesicular monoamine transporters (VMATs) are expressed exclusively in neurons and mediate the storage of histamine and other monoamines."
7. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-Vascular_7-6) Broadley KJ (March 2010). "The vascular effects of trace amines and amphetamines". *Pharmacol. Ther*. **125** (3): 363–375. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1016/j.pharmthera.2009.11.005](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.pharmthera.2009.11.005). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [19948186](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19948186). "Trace amines are metabolized in the mammalian body via monoamine oxidase (MAO; EC 1.4.3.4) (Berry, 2004) (Fig. 2) ... It deaminates primary and secondary amines that are free in the neuronal cytoplasm but not those bound in storage vesicles of the sympathetic neurone ... Similarly, β-PEA would not be deaminated in the gut as it is a selective substrate for MAO-B which is not found in the gut ...  
    Brain levels of endogenous trace amines are several hundred-fold below those for the classical neurotransmitters noradrenaline, dopamine and serotonin but their rates of synthesis are equivalent to those of noradrenaline and dopamine and they have a very rapid turnover rate (Berry, 2004). Endogenous extracellular tissue levels of trace amines measured in the brain are in the low nanomolar range. These low concentrations arise because of their very short half-life ..."
8. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Miller_8-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Miller_8-1) Miller GM (January 2011). ["The emerging role of trace amine-associated receptor 1 in the functional regulation of monoamine transporters and dopaminergic activity"](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3005101). *J. Neurochem*. **116** (2): 164–176. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1111/j.1471-4159.2010.07109.x](https://doi.org/10.1111%2Fj.1471-4159.2010.07109.x). [PMC](/wiki/PMC_(identifier) "PMC (identifier)") [3005101](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3005101). 9. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-8) [***j***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-9) [***k***](#cite_ref-Human_trace_amines_and_hTAARs_October_2016_review_9-10) Khan MZ, Nawaz W (October 2016). "The emerging roles of human trace amines and human trace amine-associated receptors (hTAARs) in central nervous system". *Biomed. Pharmacother*. **83**: 439–449. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1016/j.biopha.2016.07.002](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.biopha.2016.07.002). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [27424325](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27424325).
10. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Renaissance_GPCR_10-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Renaissance_GPCR_10-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-Renaissance_GPCR_10-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-Renaissance_GPCR_10-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-Renaissance_GPCR_10-4) Lindemann L, Hoener MC (May 2005). "A renaissance in trace amines inspired by a novel GPCR family". *Trends Pharmacol. Sci*. **26** (5): 274–281. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1016/j.tips.2005.03.007](https://doi.org/10.1016%2Fj.tips.2005.03.007). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [15860375](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15860375). "In addition to the main metabolic pathway, TAs can also be converted by nonspecific N-methyltransferase (NMT) [22] and phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase (PNMT) [23] to the corresponding secondary amines (e.g. synephrine [14], N-methylphenylethylamine and N-methyltyramine [15]), which display similar activities on TAAR1 (TA1) as their primary amine precursors...Both dopamine and 3-methoxytyramine, which do not undergo further N-methylation, are partial agonists of TAAR1 (TA1). ...  
    The dysregulation of TA levels has been linked to several diseases, which highlights the corresponding members of the TAAR family as potential targets for drug development. In this article, we focus on the relevance of TAs and their receptors to nervous system-related disorders, namely schizophrenia and depression; however, TAs have also been linked to other diseases such as migraine, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, substance abuse and eating disorders [7,8,36]. Clinical studies report increased β-PEA plasma levels in patients suffering from acute schizophrenia [37] and elevated urinary excretion of β-PEA in paranoid schizophrenics [38], which supports a role of TAs in schizophrenia. As a result of these studies, β-PEA has been referred to as the body's 'endogenous amphetamine' [39]"
